title: Impact Strength Index
aim: To determine the Impact strength index of rock .
theory: Impact Strength Index test is used to determine the resistance of rock to impact loading.
It evaluates how a rock behaves under sudden dynamic loads such as hammer blows.
The test is based on the principle of energy required to break a rock sample by impact.
A standard hammer is dropped from a fixed height onto the rock specimen.
The rock undergoes repeated impacts until it breaks into smaller pieces.
The number of blows required to cause failure indicates the strength of the rock.
Stronger rocks require more blows, while weaker rocks break with fewer blows.
The test measures the toughness and resistance to sudden fracture.
It is particularly useful in mining, blasting, and excavation studies.
The index is expressed as an Impact Strength Index value based on number of blows or energy.
The test is simple, quick, and suitable for comparative analysis of rocks.
It helps in selecting suitable equipment and excavation methods in rock engineering.
procedure: Take the rock sample and break it into pieces of size about 12–16 mm.
Weigh about 100 g of the sample accurately.
Place the sample in a standard cylindrical steel container of the impact testing apparatus.
Use a hammer of about 2 kg weight.
Raise the hammer to a fixed height of 30 cm (300 mm).
Allow the hammer to fall freely onto the sample.
Repeat the process for a fixed number of blows (usually 20 blows).
After applying all blows, collect the crushed material carefully.
Sieve the material using a standard sieve (generally 2.36 mm or 2 mm IS sieve).
Measure the weight of fines passing through the sieve.
Calculate the Impact Strength Index using number of blows, initial weight, and weight of fines produced.
precautions:
• The cylinder must be throughly cleanded.
  • The screw cap must be closed tightly.
result: The PSI is 333kgf/cm^2 and 180kgf/cm^2 for sample 1 and Sample 2 respectively.
